case_name: ST v Maidstone and Tunbridge Wells NHS Trust [2015] EWHC 51 (QB) (16 January 2015)

summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The Claimant suffers from at least one, and probably two, congenital haematological conditions.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On the evening of 24 November 2004, the Claimant was unwell and was taken to hospital where blood samples were taken.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Defendant has admitted that, by failing to admit the Claimant to hospital on the evening of 24 November and by delaying performance of the blood transfusion until mid-morning the following day, it was negligent.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On 27 November 2004, the Claimant suffered seizures. He was found to have had a series of strokes, as a result of which he suffered brain damage and has been left with severe and permanent disability.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr 'Kling' Chong gave evidence for the Claimant. He is a Consultant in Paediatric Neuroradiology at Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children, London.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Dr Wellesley St. Clair Forbes who, until his retirement from NHS practice in May 2009, worked as a Consultant Neuroradiologist at Salford Royal Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: I found the Claimant's Neuroradiologist, Dr Chong, to be a fair, balanced and impressive witness.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Dr Becker was plainly a very experienced clinician. However, he relied on the 1992 edition (rather than the most recent 2011 edition) of an authoritative Textbook.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Dr Telfer's evidence was in some respects unsatisfactory.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Dr O'Callaghan's evidence was that there are few proven risk factors for stroke in children.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: I am satisfied, on a balance of probabilities, that the primary cause of the Claimant's strokes was a focal cerebral arteriopathy which was in turn caused by the URTI from which he was suffering.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Claimant has not succeeded in establishing his primary case.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Claimant's secondary case must fail.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Unwell with noticeable jaundice, tiredness, and complaints of pain
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: The patient was unwell with a cough and cold, became tired, lost appetite, and started to become noticeably jaundiced a few days before 24 November 2004.

requestables.name: Bilirubin Level
requestables.description: Measurement of bilirubin in the blood

requestables.name: CT Scan of Brain
requestables.description: CT scan to assess for neurological complications

reveal.detailed_findings: Imaging performed but findings not detailed in judgment

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to hospital with severe symptoms

key_events.event: Blood samples taken, very low Hb level noted

timeline_phases.name: Hospital Workup
timeline_phases.description: Patient assessed and managed in hospital

timeline_phases.name: Critical Decision Phase
timeline_phases.description: Decisions regarding transfusion and fluid management

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in hospital with severe anaemia and dehydration
decision_points.prompt: What should be the immediate management for severe anaemia?

options.clinical_reasoning: Urgent transfusion needed due to very low Hb level

options.description: Delay transfusion until the next day
options.clinical_reasoning: Delay not justified given the severity of anaemia

actual_action_defendant.description: Transfusion delayed until the next day
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Hospital practice not to transfuse after 22.00hrs

expected_action_court.description: Immediate transfusion should have been performed
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated urgency due to low Hb

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in transfusion led to worsening condition

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with severe anaemia and dehydration

expected_action_court.description: IV fluids should have been administered

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to administer IV fluids contributed to dehydration
explanation.what_should_have_happened: IV fluids should have been given to manage dehydration

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered seizures and strokes, resulting in severe and permanent disability

key_findings: Breach of duty in delaying transfusion and failing to administer IV fluids
Causation established between delay and strokes

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to promptly transfuse and manage dehydration
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Immediate transfusion and fluid management could have prevented strokes

factual_timeline.event: Patient presents to hospital with severe anaemia

allegations.allegation: Failure to promptly transfuse and manage dehydration
